frameworks.name: bounded-memory reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: reward sensitivity constrained by limited memory capacity over action values

frameworks.name: optimal perseveration
frameworks.one_line: perseverative bias as efficient policy given finite memory cost

frameworks.name: rate-distortion
frameworks.one_line: trade-off between reward gain and memory cost frames efficiency deviations

core_question: Does anhedonia reflect reduced memory capacity or suboptimal perseveration given that capacity, and does KOR antagonism alter these components?
key_assumptions: reward insensitivity can arise from limited memory capacity
limited capacity makes perseveration toward frequent actions adaptive
deviations from optimal perseveration constitute inefficiency
manipulations: clinical group: anhedonic vs control across four datasets
pharmacological: KOR antagonist vs placebo (RCT secondary analysis)
key_findings: anhedonia linked to efficiency deficits but not memory capacity deficits
KOR antagonism increases both memory capacity and efficiency
perseverative bias dissociates into capacity-driven vs efficiency-driven components
interpretations: anhedonic reward insensitivity reflects inefficient use of memory, not its scarcity
KOR antagonism has cognitively distinct effects, only one of which maps onto anhedonia
